diagrams.title: Basic Structure Chart - Module Hierarchy
diagrams.description: A fundamental structure chart showing the hierarchical relationship between modules in a system. The root module represents the main program, with child modules showing the decomposition of functionality. Arrows indicate calling relationships, with the arrow direction showing which module calls which.
elements: Calling arrows (solid lines with filled arrowheads)
diagrams.purpose: Explains the fundamental concept of structure charts showing how a system is decomposed into modules in a top-down hierarchical manner

diagrams.title: Structure Chart with Data and Control Symbols
diagrams.description: An extended structure chart demonstrating data couples (data passed between modules) and control indicators. Shows how data flows between modules using arrow notations with circles for data couples and small circles with letters for control signals.
elements: Data arrows with filled circles (data couples)
Control arrows with unfilled circles (control signals)
Iteration symbol (curved arrow with double line)
diagrams.purpose: Teaches the notation for showing data flow and control flow between modules, including iteration and selection constructs

diagrams.title: Complete Structure Chart Example - Payroll System
diagrams.description: A comprehensive structure chart for a payroll system showing real-world module decomposition. Demonstrates practical application with multiple levels of modules, showing how a business application is structured with modules for employee data, time recording, calculations, and reporting.
elements: Level 1 modules: Get Employee Data, Process Time Records, Calculate Pay, Generate Reports
Level 2 modules under Calculate Pay: Calculate Regular Hours, Calculate Overtime, Calculate Deductions
Data couples (arrows with circles showing data like hours, rates, deductions)
Control flags (arrows with hollow circles showing control signals)
Library modules (rectangles with double vertical lines)
diagrams.purpose: Provides a complete, realistic example of a structure chart for a payroll system, demonstrating how real software systems are structured and decomposed into manageable modules
